data.title: Salesforce: A Narrative Issue
data.description:
Salesforce (CRM) is an enterprise software company that provides solutions to companies workflows, with the aim of automating and streamlining day-to-day operations, which increases efficiency for enterprises. Salesforce continues to trade at lower prices, as growth rates as well as investor sentiment have been affected by the rise of AI, as one of the use cases of AI could perhaps make Salesforce's purpose obsolete. The enterprise workflow solutions companies as a whole continue to provide services, or at least try to provide services, that now include AI, in order for them to create value and higher productivity for workflows, such as Workday acquiring Paradox, which essentially uses AI to reshape the hiring process for companies. With the stock continuing to get beaten down as well as an unclear future, despite optimistic numbers for 2030, I would rather see results before being too optimistic, as AI seems to be a threat for many software companies, including Salesforce. Hence, I'm rating Salesforce at a 'Hold.'

What does Salesforce actually do?

Salesforce provides the full set of workflow solutions that a company demands, providing the technology for managing all of the company's relationships and interactions with customers and potential customers. Salesforce also offers a lot of functions that a company may need, such as their Data 360 (used to be called Data Cloud), analytics (Agentforce), and marketing.

Salesforce can also automate workflows like marketing workflows, as well as simply providing all a company needs when it comes to managing their own workflows, providing the software and system for them to thrive and increase productivity, rather than relying on manual work. With the rise of AI and agentic agents, Salesforce is now also transitioning to offering such services with Agentforce 360, which aims to provide a better experience to users.

With their own projections of over 40% of Fortune 1000 companies work being elevated by AI by 2029, according to their investor day 2025, as Salesforce seems to be bullish in the near future, but the stock price has spoken a whole different story in the recent years as the threat of AI towards Salesforce has created huge negative sentiment around the stock and underperformance compared to the S&P 500.

Thesis

Salesforce continues to be underappreciated in the market, underperforming on every single time scale up to 10 years. As Salesforce continues to get underappreciated, it continues to trade at a lower and lower premium, as Salesforce's stock continues to seem cheaper with every dip and decent earnings report.

I believe that the current narrative surrounding Salesforce is rather bearish, with AI potentially displacing Salesforce's value and essentially making it obsolete. Salesforce's core job of saving and running huge amounts of data is supposedly easily replaced by AI chatbots and LLMs, as LLMs are only getting better by the day. Using LLMs for workflows may be way better and cheaper than Salesforce. Salesforce's reply is Agentforce, but it is still at a relatively early stage, which hasn't yielded great results for Salesforce yet.

I believe it is now all about execution and changing people's narratives surrounding the company. From their investor day, they projected over $60B in revenue in 2030 alone, which makes their current valuations very cheap and attractive. However, until Salesforce is able to show the trend towards the bullish and optimistic number, markets and investors remain conservative, as Salesforce's stock price hasn't really moved on a 5 year scale. If Salesforce is able to execute well with Agentforce being widely adopted, Salesforce would trade at a higher premium, especially considering its current levels. Until the inflection point is clearer for Salesforce to trend upwards, I remain neutral on Salesforce, as their whole business model could be obsolete with the existence of AI chatbots and LLMs.

Salesforce's Stickiness

Enterprise workflow solutions software companies all tend to have a very similar moat, with its largest strength being high stickiness. When an enterprise integrates its operations into one of these workflows, so much data is integrated, from payroll, details on individuals and transactions. Due to this, moving data from one enterprise workflow to another would take time and would cause huge time lags as well as operations coming to a halt. Due to this, it simply wouldn't be rational for a firm to move over operations, considering operations would get affected, so the retention and renewal rate for these enterprise workflow platforms is high. There is even evidence now that many enterprises are unwilling to move over to newer platforms (SAP 4/HANA problem, which will be explored in the Concerns section), which shows that even a better, more advanced platform doesn't matter. Perhaps enterprises are too sticky, which could be a concern for these firms, as adoption of AI agents could be slower, unless the difference in productivity is so clear and very noticeable.

There isn't any recent data on the retention/renewal rate from Salesforce, with 2022's renewal rate around the 93% mark. From other workflow software companies like ServiceNow and Workday, their retention rates are at 98% and 97%, respectively, essentially showing that once their platforms are used, they essentially become embedded into a company's operation with huge stickiness.

Salesforce AI Potential

The next few quarters for Salesforce are very important, in order to highlight the progress of Agentforce and whether they are able to meet their 2030 projections with Agentforce being one of the key catalysts for their $60B revenue by FY30, which makes its current levels seem rather cheap. As seen above for the use cases, one of the use cases is to reduce time to first sale by up to 33%, increasing productivity overall.

Their business continues to grow with explosive growth rates, with their Data 360 and AI ARR at over $1B. With Agentic AI only at an ARR of $440M, it needs to continue to grow at such high rates to boost the narrative that AI won't displace Salesforce, but rather be able to provide even better services with AI that cannot be displaced for operations. Agentforce is still at a very speculative stage and far from being mature, which is a positive and a negative for investors. Salesforce has spent a huge amount on Agentforce, such as the acquisition of Informatica, which in their own recent press release when they completed the acquisition:

establishing a unified and comprehensive data foundation for agentic AI — and enabling AI agents to operate safely, responsibly, and at scale across the modern enterprise.

“You have to get your data right to get your AI right,” said Marc Benioff, Chair and CEO of Salesforce. “Data and context is the true fuel of Agentforce, and without clean, connected, trusted data there is no intelligence – only hallucination. Informatica is the trusted platform that turns fragmented enterprise data into context, so every agent can reason, act, and deliver outcomes with precision. When companies get their data right, they get their AI right, and Agentforce becomes unstoppable.”

Spending approximately $8B, it shows that Salesforce is truly committed to not being displaced by AI, but rather utilizing AI for the next step of the company. This obviously makes it very exciting for Salesforce's future, but with how the stock has reacted over the last year, it can be said that investors remain rather conservative on the future of Agentforce, considering chatbots could do what Agentforce does and potentially even better and cheaper. At its current price level and with an AI catalyst, I see the bullish thesis for Salesforce with Agentforce going smoothly and according to target, but there is certainly a bearish possibility where these enterprise workflow solution companies become obsolete, or at least have an alternative for enterprises that is potentially cheaper and better.

Most projected metrics for FY26 have been raised, while the projected GAAP EPS and operating margins are being cut. The investment towards AI is clearly taking a hit, and now the question is whether they can continue to grow to their targets whilst maintaining/expanding margins, which a better picture will be given next quarter. With the Informatica acquisition now closed, focus on questions in the next earnings call, which isn't too far away, as management could shed some more light on the integration of Informatica with Salesforce, and what it will turn out to be—essentially some form of roadmap for clarity to investors. I believe the next few quarters, starting from the next one, will be important for the trajectory of Salesforce, as any numbers on how Agentforce is doing and signs of potential acceleration will show investors that Agentforce can compete with chatbots in the future.

I believe that Salesforce is stuck in a narrative issue, which is honestly a common theme for many big names like Duolingo (AI displacement), Amazon (AWS slowdown), and Adobe (AI displacement) in the market right now, as indexes trade near all-time highs while these stocks are rather flat YTD or near 52-week lows. If Salesforce can get the narrative changing again, as it continues to trade at lower and lower premiums, Salesforce's stock could easily recover, but as long as it continues to have AI displacement fears, and Agentforce continues to be at a speculative/early stage, it is very likely that Salesforce will continue to be stagnant/decline.

Concerns

This is an idea that is in most of my articles on workflow enterprise solution companies, where the stickiness may perhaps be too sticky, so perhaps all their moats are to contain their customers and prevent them from leaving, which essentially leads to no one dominant firm in the industry. Since Salesforce's offerings don't really have a huge differentiation from other workflows, it is unlikely for Salesforce's core offerings to accelerate anytime soon, which means the stock's lower valuations may be justified since the explosive growth is already over, as Salesforce is too mature.

In more detail in my SAP article, 60% of SAP customers still use their legacy platform and have no intention of switching, as Gartner calls the switching deadline unrealistic. The original deadline was this year, with the deadline now at 2027.

This is a very odd issue that could also potentially reflect how well Agentforce might perform. The above issue is clearly a SAP one, but to me signals 2 things. First, the platform obviously has huge stickiness, so Salesforce is unlikely to get those enterprises business. Second, it is clear that users are content with the platform that has been provided, which raises the question: do enterprises need a better platform, or simply how many enterprises can utilize AI and actually find that it creates value or is more productive?

There is no doubt that Salesforce is trying to position itself for the future, but though we are rather early into this AI infrastructure buildout, understanding use cases of AI and the rapid improvement of LLMs, Salesforce is not ready now, but when it is ready in a few years, I have doubts that a chatbot wouldn't be able to replicate what Salesforce does, which essentially means there is a huge stickiness now, but perhaps not in the near future.

This essentially rolls into the concern that AI will displace Salesforce in the future, as instead of having full-on systems and software, LLMs will get so powerful that they will be able to accurately store data as well as provide data whenever the individual needs it. Rather than having systems on payroll and stuff, just typing in a simple command to show 'employee details' could essentially make Salesforce sort of obsolete. A lot of polishing will still be required, obviously, but I'm just trying to create the scenario with AI taking off and becoming perfect and enterprises increasing productivity with these chatbots, while Salesforce is in the list of one of the companies made obsolete by AI. I'm being rather bearish here, but with the stock's performance on every time scale up to 10 years, I believe that there is a good proportion of investors/analysts who believe this narrative/scenario as the stock continues to get beaten down.

Valuation

Salesforce has a current market cap of $222B, which, assuming it achieves its targets of $60B revenue by FY30, means that its P/S will be around 3.7 looking forward by over 4 years and 2 quarters.

With their historical P/S TTM at 7.52, if Salesforce trades at their historical averages by FY30 at this P/S assuming $60B in revenue, that would mean just over a double in stock price over 4 years and 2 months. That will likely outperform the S&P 500 at such rates, but the question now is how well they can actually execute. As their valuations across all metrics are trading considerably below historical averages by a significant margin, understandably due to the threat of AI, the question now is whether Salesforce can recover and overcome the AI problem or get beaten down by AI. This is why the next few quarters are so important, as Salesforce has set the stage for how it will perform over the coming years, so expectations for the first few quarters to be on its trajectory are very important for the future of Salesforce.

Salesforce now trades at the other lowest amongst some of its closest peers on a P/S basis, as the stock has lacked momentum in the last year. Salesforce does look cheap compared to the relative market as well as historical averages, but it is up to the investor to decide whether Salesforce can execute and utilize AI, or slowly become obsolete as LLMs can provide a way better service for cheaper. As I already mentioned, the stock needs a narrative change to indicate that it has a position in the future. Buying a piece of the company is believing that it will operate successfully in the future, as Salesforce's future is rather uncertain as of right now, which leads to lower premiums.

Final Thoughts

I'm rather bullish on AI and the use cases for LLMs in the future, so I'm slightly bearish on certain software companies like Salesforce, as I exited my position fully months back when Salesforce failed to recover after the April lows, while names in my portfolio like AMD recovered and recovered fast. Since then, there has been a narrative issue with Salesforce, as the stock has been stagnant and fluctuating around the $230s and $240s levels.

Valuations for Salesforce are rather cheap right now, as the future of Agentforce is certainly very exciting, but I'm staying away for now. I have already mentioned numerous times, and perhaps too many times, that Salesforce has a narrative issue. I'm not betting on the potential change of narrative, as this issue is most likely solved with one quarter of earnings showing that Agentforce has a place in the future, which could happen in any of the next quarters, especially now with Informatica, but I would rather stay on the sidelines than own Salesforce, to trade or to hold. If Salesforce continues not to change the narrative that AI could displace them and make them obsolete, it is likely that Salesforce will continue to trade like dead money, simply flat/trading lower.

data.title: Trying To Give A Credit Rating For OFS Credit Company
data.description:
In today's article, we will delve deeper into CLO CEFs. More specifically, we will try to step into Moody's shoes and give a credit rating to such a company that primarily invests in CLO equity securities. It is about OFS Credit Company Inc. (OCCI) - we will look at the fund and give it a credit rating, but not according to the CEFs methodology. You may wonder why, since OCCI is a CEF. While that is true, Moody's methodology is not designed for CLO CEFs, and the one that comes closest to our fund is the Moody's BDC methodology. Readers should take the credit scoring with a grain of salt. OCCI is not a BDC, and the credit agencies don't have a methodology to evaluate these CEFs. We are just seeing quite correlated moves in the BDC and CEF CLO space and want to make a simplified comparison.

OCCI Basic Info

We will briefly introduce you to the fund with a few basic details.

  • Asset under management: close to $148 million
  • Portfolio:

  • Industry exposure:

  • Holdings: The number of holdings is close to 75. Below are shown the top 10 of them:

  • Expense ratio: 12.43%
  • Non-leverage expense ratio: 8.2%
  • Management fees: 2.8%
  • Historical performance: The 5-year total return is close to 5%:

  • The common stock is currently trading at $4.51, with a close to 20% discount:

This chart is no joke. When a company loses 30% in a year, it cannot be viewed as a positive for any part of the capital structure. Something bad is happening.

Now let's get to the real part...

Credit rating for OCCI

Moody's considers six key factors in its BDC approach when evaluating the companies: Profitability, Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion, Asset Quality- Problem loans and Senior Secured loans, Cash flow and liquidity- Debt Structure and Secured debt. You can see them below in the picture:

Profitability

To assess profitability, Moody's uses a 3-year average net income-to-average-assets-under-management ratio. OCCI has approximately a negative 0.35% for this metric, so it has to take the 17 score on the Moody's scale, which is equivalent to a Caa1 credit rating.

Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio Cushion)

The regulatory limit for the asset coverage ratio for CEFs is 200%. The higher the coverage ratio, the better the credit score. The leverage of OCCI is around 43%. We calculated the asset coverage ratio cushion to be 17%. This is also one of the most important factors when assessing the company's overall credit score. The higher the ACRC, the better, as this simply means less leverage. The score we get here is 11, or a Ba1 equivalent. Keep in mind that we are using the regulatory limit for CEFs rather than BDCs, as the methodology states that other covenants may further reduce the asset coverage cushion.

Asset Quality

The company has no problem loans or senior secured debt, but here we will assign both metrics the worst credit rating on the Moody's scale, since OCCI mainly invests in CLO equity, which, as we know, is the riskiest tranche there is in the CLO securitization.

Cash flow and liquidity

OCCI has no secured debt whatsoever, which gives it another top score on the scale, or the equivalent of Aaa. This is the only decent score OCCI gets based on the methodology. Given that its assets generate high current income, OCCI mostly suffers from NAV deterioration rather than liquidity problems. This is why we give it an investment-grade liquidity score. Even a Ponzi scheme gets a high liquidity score at first, which is kind of funny.

Overall Score

The overall credit score of OCCI is equivalent to Ba1. But we also adjust the credit score based on market capitalization, premium to NAV, historical yield spreads, and management fees. Taking all these, OCCI gets a final score equivalent to a Ba3 credit rating:

I personally prefer a credit score that is better distributed among its constituents. When you have 3 categories below the Ca mark, the absence of secured debt fails to compensate. It is also quite important to note that at some point in the past, we were heavily invested in CEF CLO baby bonds and preferred stocks. One of the main reasons for doing so was the fact that the public used to treat the business model quite favorably, giving premium valuations to the common stocks. At that time, all our adjustments were lifting the credit score. We depended on our hedging reaction of shorting the common stock trading at an unjustified premium. Currently, this hedging reaction is gone, with the sector equity trading at double-digit discounts.

Conclusion

OCCI is a fund that invests primarily in CLO equity, and as such carries a higher risk than standard BDCs or corporate bonds. If we evaluate it according to Moody’s methodology for BDC companies, the overall score indicates Ba3, but at the lower end of that category, mainly due to the riskier assets in the portfolio. One can add their own understanding and evaluate credibility in a different way, resulting in a credit score that is much higher or much lower. Even within our team, our subjective assessments range from high investment grade to complete trash, depending on the person's bias.

data.title: SCHD: Stop Calling It A Value Fund; It's A Quality Momentum Strategy
data.description:
Thesis

Here's what bothers me about how most investors talk about the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). They call it a boring dividend fund or maybe a defensive value play. They’ll see its lower P/E ratio (~17.5x), focus on established companies, and stable yield (currently ~3.8%), and move on. This presents a comfortable, easy, but an incomplete view.

After analyzing how SCHD's underlying Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index (DJUSDIV) works, I've come to a different conclusion. SCHD is not designed to be a fixed basket of cheap, high-yielding stocks expected to compound slowly. Instead, it follows an index that uses an unemotional, rules based quantitative approach to identify high quality dividend paying companies with fundamental momentum.

I am not talking about price momentum here - that market obsession with stocks going up because they increased yesterday. I’m talking about Quality Momentum: the acceleration of actual business, profitability, and cash flow to shareholders.

The market is currently treating SCHD as a stagnant portfolio of aging value stocks, but in fact its portfolio represents the strong leaders of tomorrow.

What is the Quality Momentum Approach?

The simplicity of SCHD hides a very efficient quantitative process. Calling it a passive fund is unfair to the alpha it delivers. Let me walk you through the steps the index follows to select stocks with quality momentum.

Firstly, a company needs a track record of 10 years of dividend payments in a row before the index considers other measures like valuation, yield, or momentum. This does not just act as a dividend screen; it filters durability. This requirement instantly excludes recent IPOs, companies that suspended dividends during crises such as COVID-19, and those that focus only on growth rather than returns to shareholders.

What is left are businesses with solid cash flow and management discipline of paying back to shareholders across economic cycles.

The index then ranks these companies on dividend yield with an aim to select the top 100 based on 4 fundamental factors:

Cash Flow to Total Debt: Eliminates leverage risk and zombie companies

Return on Equity ("ROE"): Pure quality and capital efficiency metric

Dividend Yield: Ensures meaningful current income

5-Year Dividend Growth Rate: The secret sauce

This step filters for capital efficiency. It narrows the focus to companies that aren't just durable. These companies are generating so much extra cash that returning it to shareholders does not put any strain on the balance sheet.

The final measure (5-Yr Dividend Growth) is, in my view, the most important and misunderstood thing about SCHD. It is a lagging but very strong factor for selecting stocks whose fundamental momentum is accelerating.

Why is this important? Because a company cannot achieve high dividend growth without having recently experienced a sustained period of increasing free cash flow. Also, it shows the management’s confidence in the business outlook as they are aggressively raising payout.

When you combine high ROE with high recent dividend growth, you are not just buying quality or value. You are buying companies with quality momentum that are earning high returns, generating cash, and sharing more with shareholders.

What the Market Got Wrong About March 2025

The index reconstitutes annually and rebalances quarterly. The March 2025 reconstitution provides a perfect case study to test this quality momentum thesis. The market's reaction has been mostly negative, with some saying its bet on energy made the fund too cyclical, and some saying removing companies like Pfizer (PFE) was a mistake.

But look at these changes through the quality momentum framework, and you’ll see a completely different picture. The index was not making sector bets; it was simply looking for companies where solid cash flow currently exists.

It selected energy companies because after years of capital discipline post-2014, their business models have transformed. For e.g. the 5-year CAGR of dividends of ConocoPhillips (COP) is 13.5%. Its ROE, cash flow, and balance sheet also improved greatly over the last decade. The index saw these companies as quality businesses with accelerating shareholder returns. That resulting in 21% energy exposure is not a sectoral bet; rather, it's the direct outcome of its approach.

For those wondering what if the exposure increases further. As the Energy sector is cyclical and is directly correlated with commodity prices, this could negatively impact the performance of SCHD. But here’s the thing: the index rule says that no single sector can represent more than 25% of the index. So, I don't see concentration risk becoming a real issue.

Actually, it has already come down to around 19.5%, and based on current commodity prices, I expect the December 2025 rebalance to further decrease it to around 17-18%. That would bring its maximum sectoral weightings back to the historical range while maintaining quality momentum.

The removal of regional banks also wasn't a call on interest rates or recession risk. Their dividend growth rate has declined. Their quality momentum was turning negative. The index simply did its job by rotating away from decelerating momentum, regardless of the sector.

Pfizer's business was also decelerating. Its Covid vaccine revenues collapsed, loss of exclusivity ("LOE") wave was approaching, and dividend growth slowed. Compare this to Merck (MRK), which is also facing LOE, but its blockbuster drug Keytruda is still doing great, sales up 18% in FY’24, and has years of runway remaining. Their dividend increased by 5.4% in 2024 compared to only 2.4% for Pfizer.

Some might view this as a healthcare reshuffle, but again, the framework made a usual rotation from decelerating momentum to accelerating momentum.

Look, I believe in Pfizer’s transformation story, but the algorithm does not care about narratives. It bets on companies already showing quality momentum and reallocates capital into those high-quality high-cash-flow companies.

Why SCHD's Pain Is Actually An Opportunity

SCHD's recent performance has been rough. Year to date it has returned only 1.4% in total compared to SPDR S&P 500 ETF's (SPY) 13%. For the last 12 months it has returned -2.3%, and since 2023 it has significantly lagged the S&P 500 because of the AI-driven tech rally.

I know this underperformance might frustrate some investors but understanding why it's happening can help us recognize the investment opportunity.

As the index reconstitutes only once per year based on trailing data. This creates a structural timing challenge. During the 2023 reconstitution, the algorithm looked at 2022 data and saw energy with solid cash flows (thanks to the 2022 commodity spike), tech stocks beaten down during the 2022 bear market, and financials appearing less favorable after various banks collapsed in early 2023.

It made changes for that environment, but then, AI rally began. SCHD was now locked into a value heavy portfolio for 12 months while the market moved in a different direction.

Again, during the March 2025 reconstitution, it shifted towards energy and away from the financial sector. And this time, tariff and recession concerns made energy companies temporarily unloved.

This does not mean the methodology is flawed. It shows a disciplined, rebalancing process that does not follow price momentum. The question is, do you think fundamentals are going to matter again, or is this time really different?

You should know that this pattern is not new to SCHD. During the 2018-2019 late-cycle growth rally, it underperformed the S&P 500. But during the 2022 bear market, it did much better than both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq exactly because of its disciplined nature.

Its 12.4% average annual return since inception (roughly 15 yr) was not delivered by winning every quarter. It was done by buying quality companies at favorable prices and holding them through various cycles.

It is this feature of SCHD that leads to poor performance during speculative rallies because the methodology filters out narrative driven businesses.

For example, the market narrative for Palantir (PLTR) is defense tech disruptor, trades at a forward P/E of over 230x, and has given a return of nearly 180% in the last 12 months. But SCHD’s filter eliminates it immediately because of no dividend history.

The market might be right about its long-term potential. But for SCHD to include you, prove your business model works, prove you can return cash to shareholders, and then do it for 10 years.

Take NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) as another example. The market narrative for NVIDIA is an essential AI infra disruptor. It saw exceptional growth, and has been paying a little dividend since 2012. But initially SCHD filtered it out as it lacked a 10-year dividend history. When NVIDIA finally qualified for SCHD, its valuation already seemed to carry a high speculative premium, which might have triggered other fundamental filters to exclude it.

This filtering approach proved its worth during the 2022 bear market, which was like a stress test.

As both Nasdaq and S&P 500 saw huge drawdowns, SCHD was barely impacted. As other portfolios were down significantly, SCHD’s dividends were not only stable, but they grew at a double-digit rate. The filter did what it was designed to do: keep investors capital out of the speculative bets.

This makes SCHD a powerful tool for various types of portfolio construction that can help take risk elsewhere. For example, a meaningful allocation (20-25%) of your overall portfolio into SCHD can provide the defensive cushion and income to navigate through the volatility from thematic bets. So, if your AI thesis fails, you still have a meaningful compounding element in the portfolio.

Looking forward, SCHD's dividend growth rate seems to be well positioned to accelerate as energy cash flows compound and recent additions mature. Energy companies like ConocoPhillips and Chevron have already announced dividend increases. Based on this, I expect SCHD's dividend growth rate to increase from the current ~6% to 8-9% by 2026.

Importantly, if we enter a period of slower economic growth (benefits defensive positioning), persistent inflation (benefits energy and pricing power), market volatility (benefits low-beta, cash generating businesses), these could act as macro tailwinds for SCHD. Considering the overextended valuation of the S&P 500 and NASDAQ, SCHD seems to be well positioned for the next fundamentally driven cycle.

Valuation Analysis

Based on their P/E multiple, SCHD is trading at a ~30 percent discount to the SPY, and over a 32 percent discount to its peer, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation (VIG). As it picks companies with similar or higher ROE, accelerating fundamental momentum, solid balance sheets, and durability, this forms a good asymmetric return profile.

I expect the valuation gap to decrease over time as the market will ultimately appreciate the quality momentum like characteristics of the portfolio. The 3.8% yield, which is ~3.5 times that of the SPY’s 1.1%, should fully cover opportunity cost while waiting for the next cycle.

Why do I think this is the best time for long-term investors? Because during low cyclical sentiments, the investor becomes frustrated and underestimation occurs. It is currently trading near the midpoint of its 52-week range and nearly 10% below its all time high, which offers good entries to those investors having a longer-term horizon. The best moment to buy a contrarian, disciplined strategy is exactly when it has led to a short-term underachievement.

The current valuation discount is simply too extreme given the portfolio's quality characteristics. Assuming SCHD's P/E expands from ~17.5x to just 20x (still below SPY) over 2-3 years as quality recognition returns or because mean reversion occurs. And combining this with dividend growth of 7% annually with a yield of 3.8%, it results in annualized total returns of 15-18%.

In the worst-case scenario, even if P/E expansion doesn't happen and we only see dividend growth at a much slower rate of 4%, it is still an 8-10% annualized return. This is quite reasonable for a low-volatility strategy.

Although the worst case has possibility in the near term, especially if the AI rally persists or the energy prices drop drastically, the base case is much more favorable in terms of risk/reward for long-term investors.

Risks to Thesis

The primary risk to my Quality Momentum thesis is a prolonged speculative rally where valuations detach from fundamentals for an extended period, similar to the dot-com bubble. In that case, SCHD's discipline would become a performance drag. However, with the market now trading at historically high levels, the asymmetry seems to favor SCHD.

SCHD's approach takes time because it is designed to deliver performance over a multi-year cycle, not in a single quarter. Those investors who abandon the strategy during an AI driven rally may miss the next unavoidable rotation when fundamentals become decisive again.

Also, since the factors it considers for filtering are backward looking (e.g. 10-year dividend history), it can be slow to respond to new trends. So I don't expect it to be the first to own the next groundbreaking company. Investors should be okay with this conservative and gradual approach and understand that because of this, they will miss opportunities like NVIDIA.

Investor Takeaway

The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF is a low-cost, highly accessible quantitative strategy available to retail investors. It uses an unemotional, rules-based approach to identify stocks with Quality Momentum.

SCHD offers investors a rare opportunity to buy an advanced strategy at a very low valuation because of the market's current obsession with price momentum in tech. With SCHD you're buying a portfolio of proven winners that are getting stronger, just before their next recognition phase.

The 2 years of frustrating underperformance weren't random. It resulted from the disciplined approach: that annual reconstitution process being temporarily out of sync with a speculative market. For investors willing to look ahead, SCHD represents a compelling asymmetric opportunity because I believe that same discipline is what will drive outperformance when fundamentals reassert themselves.

I have a Buy rating on the SCHD ETF with an expected 15-18% annualized return in 2-3 years.

data.title: Novo's Worst Year Is Your Best Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

On the surface, Novo Nordisk (NVO) looks like a great "growth at a reasonable price" opportunity (aka GARP) because its fundamentals keep improving, and the stock price still can't find its bottom, updating new lows each quarter since mid-2024:

But the attractiveness of Novo Nordisk has been present for many months, and all attempts to buy the dip in this stock turned into a nightmare for value-seeking GARP investors. Political concerns over US drug tariffs, the persisting pricing pressure on NVO's key drug (Wegovy), and rising competition amid disappointing trial data for their next-generation obesity drug (CagriSema) led to a sharp cut in guidance and a change at the top management level, which added to the stock's ugly price action.

However, by normal pharma standards, Novo's growth is projected to be superior even after all the negatives in hand. Yes, their near‑monopoly hypergrowth in obesity with premium pricing is in the past, and that has caused the valuation reset, but I believe that reset has gone too far now. Once 2025 is over, and NVO's US market share stabilizes at the new price points, the market is likely to start rewarding the firm's volume over price strategy, and multiples shouldn't get much lower than what we're seeing right now. With this core thesis in mind, I decided to rate NVO as a "Buy" stock with a solid recovery potential ahead.

Peak Pain Year For Novo

Although NVO's consolidated revenues went up by 12.47% YoY in Q3 2025, it missed the initial consensus estimate by 2.72%. The EPS showed a massive beat to consensus (~13.21%), but in comparison to last year, it was down 21.59% in US dollars, according to Seeking Alpha. As Novo was booking severance, site consolidations, and write‑downs in both its COGS and OPEX throughout the quarter, they reached only 10% in EBIT growth in 9M 2025. Without those costs, which I think were temporary in nature, the underlying EBIT would have been up by 21% (at constant currency).

Operating profit adjusted for costs related to the restructuring increased by 16% measured in Danish kroner and 21% at constant exchange rates.

[Novo Nordisk's Q3 earnings call]

A large chunk of the Q3 revenue growth was effectively spent on one‑off restructuring rather than flowing through to the bottom line, and that caused the above-mentioned drop in EPS. From what I see, 2025 has become the peak pain year for the company, as the management has guided about DKK 9 billion of restructuring and asset‑impairment charges so far.

Novo agreed to lower obesity and diabetes drug prices for Medicare and Medicaid beneficiaries in early November, and while it signals a sharp drop in ASP in the next few quarters, Novo will be able to expand the treated population over time in a meaningful way. The addressable market is expanding, and it looks like a chronic market with huge unmet needs. In Q3, NVO's gross margin was slightly above 81%, and if we assume that it drops to 70%, I see no problem in offsetting this potential dip amid no restructuring costs ahead.

What can potentially lower the COGS for Novo is its oral Wegovy formulation, which is under FDA priority review for obesity. I think so because tablets are generally cheaper and more scalable to produce and distribute compared to injectable biologics. Moving a meaningful slice of obesity volume to a high‑volume oral formulation lets Novo leverage its existing small‑molecule/tablet manufacturing infrastructure - the average selling price will drop, but the unmet demand that we see to date will likely get filled faster than we could have assumed before. And the management seems to be getting ready for a potential launch shortly after approval, likely through both traditional channels and telehealth/retail partners:

We're incredibly excited as we move closer to the approval date of the Wegovy pill. I think this is a big step forward in terms of expanding the market and for those individuals that align better with taking a pill for their obesity. We can't comment on specific pricing, of course. But what I would say is we are going to have the Wegovy pill available in all channels.

And this is different from previous launches because we will have the ability to focus on Medicaid, Medicare and commercial, but also have a cash offering available through all of our different telehealth partnerships as well as our own NovoCare Pharmacy and the retail partnerships that we've aligned as well. This is a new way to launch for us.

[Novo's Q3 Earnings call]

I'm calling 2025 a peak pain year for Novo because amid the potential serviceable market expansion shortly, the new management team is now working on cost-cutting through consolidating sites and layoffs, targeting roughly DKK 8 billion in annual savings by around 2026. So, they basically want to reach almost as much as they're projected to lose in restructuring costs in 2025. If we layer these potential savings on top of still‑growing GLP‑1 volumes and a lot of still unmet demand, plus add a more efficient manufacturing network (especially if oral Wegovy ramps), we'll get a business that can potentially expand margins even in a world of tougher pricing.

Beyond just existing drugs they sell, Novo Nordisk is quite active in M&As, as they recently bought Akero Therapeutics (for ~$5.2 billion), which looks like a natural combo fit with Wegovy in obese/metabolic‑syndrome patients. Last year, they bought Cardior Pharmaceuticals, a non‑coding RNA therapies provider for cardiovascular disease, for up to ~$1.1B billion, which might mean that they want cardiometabolic optionality around GLP‑1, according to Labiotech data. On the internal pipeline, their CagriSema, which is already in Phase 3 stage, is lining up for regulatory submission in 2026.

Novo is effectively building a multi‑asset metabolic and specialty‑disease platform, which holds a wide pipeline of promising drugs. Next year, once the cost-cutting starts to bring in some cost efficiencies, their oral Wegovy launches, and CagriSema is approved or gets closer to it, the underlying business should see at least some stabilization, and so the narrative can finally change, pulling the stock price recovery with it.

Novo's Valuation Gives A Fertile Soil For Recovery

I noticed that it's a common practice among equity research authors to compare Novo with its closest peer, Eli Lilly, when it comes to valuation - all that to say that LLY is way pricier than NVO and conclude that the latter is more attractive for value investors.

I agree on the very fact that LLY is more expensive, but Novo used to have a less broad and less diversified growth story for the investment world because Eli Lilly holds big franchises in oncology, immunology, and neurology alongside obesity (while Novo is much more concentrated in diabetes/obesity). Both stocks' multiples corrected, but LLY keeps the premium over NVO in place, and I wouldn't expect this gap to narrow significantly in the next years.

But what I suppose should happen if the narrative changes the way I described earlier is NVO's P/E recovery to at least 15-16x by the end of 2026. I'm not assuming the 10-year average of 27-28x because the growth profile shifted significantly, based on the earnings revisions. But the 15-16x range looks realistic even at EPS 5-year consensus CAGR of about 4.16%, given that there's an upside to it when the pipeline names hit the market.

At the middle of the suggested 15-16x range, NVO's stock price can adjust higher by almost 15% from today's price, even if we see 0 positive revisions made to the EPS figure. My 12-month price for NVO stock is ~$56.3, which I think may turn out to be quite conservative in case the cost cutting helps in a more meaningful way than the analysts assume, or if the firm's new drugs hit the market ahead of the guided plans.

Risks To My Views

The main risk that I see for my thesis is, of course, execution of the new executive team. I believe that next year the savings, coupled with oral Wegovy approval/launch, and a smoother path for CagriSema and other pipeline assets, will help offset the difficulties of 2025. But I may be wrong, and it can actually take many years before we see NVO coming out as a winner. The competition shouldn't be canceled out: While I'm waiting for a business recovery, Eli Lilly and smaller GLP‑1 players could continue to out-execute and grab a portion of NVO's market share in the next few years. Also, you can see from my valuation calculations that the upside potential from the current price looks capped at just 14-15%. If there's another round of earnings revisions ahead, NVO is likely to lose that upside and even get some downside.

Conclusion

I think that NVO is setting up for a reversal next year, and while I don't see a huge upside based on today's fundamentals and forward-looking consensus estimates, the undervaluation is hard to ignore. The catalysts, like aggressively cutting costs in 2026, Novo's franchise extension with oral Wegovy, as well as CagriSema aiming for a 2026 filing, are giving me hope for a better 2026 for NVO stock. I rate it as a "Buy".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMDL: Amplified Risk In A Volatile Market
data.description:
The GraniteShares 2x Long AMD Daily ETF (AMDL) is a leveraged single-stock ETF strategy designed to provide trades with exposure to 2x the daily performance of Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The strategy provided traders with 200% exposure to AMD by employing swap positions, exposing investors to certain intricacies that may impact the performance of the ETF. AMDL should only be used for daily exposure, as long-term performance may compound returns and potentially result in substantial value decay.

You can review my recent coverage of AMD here:

“AMD Sees Growth Tailwinds With Inferencing (Rating Upgrade)”

About GraniteShares 2x Long AMD Daily ETF

AMDL was launched by GraniteShares on March 4, 2024 on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy presently has a portion of the fee waived, lowering the expense ratio from 150bps to 107bps. AMDL provides ample liquidity for active trading with $491mm in net assets, with an average of $388mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, maintaining a low average bid/ask spread of 9bps.

By comparison, AMDL may be the most optimal leveraged AMD strategy on the market, providing traders both with a marketable fee and strong liquidity. Note that some of the funds below are yield-generating funds rather than leveraged strategies.

In terms of liquidity, AMDL exhibits the largest trading volumes and the largest asset base. Given that leveraged strategies are designed to be actively traded, liquidity should be a major consideration for traders when selecting the appropriate trading vehicle for a strategy.

In certain instances, AMDL’s daily performance exposure may not perfectly align with the 2x daily performance target given the number of factors that go into pricing the ETF. From the ETF’s perspective, liquidity and volatility are the two main pricing mechanisms that may lead to some price deviation. From the strategy’s perspective, interest rates and liquidity may influence the costs and pricing of the swap positions.

Looking at this product from an investment perspective, AMD may experience substantial tailwinds in the coming years driven by its partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI) for 6 GW of capacity and its growing market share of Client & Gaming x86 chips. I believe a critical factor that makes AMD stand out amongst its peers is its development of its Instinct GPUs and EPYC CPUs that can be integrated into a supercomputer, whereas market-leading peer Nvidia (NVDA) relies on 3rd parties for CPU capacity. Though Nvidia can integrate its Grace CPUs, many customers typically utilize AMD EPYC or Intel (INTC) Xeon, leading Nvidia to develop a partnership with Intel for purpose-built x86 CPUs for its next-generation AI accelerators.

Though AMD can experience significant tailwinds for its data center chips, the company’s footprint is overshadowed by the vast adoption of Nvidia deployments. Nonetheless, I believe AMD’s core x86 market can provide appealing growth, particularly as it takes on market share from Intel.

In terms of the market, I’m expecting the major hyperscalers to collectively deploy roughly $368.5b in capital for 2025, growing by 23% year-over-year in 2026. I’m expecting that capital investments in 2026 will largely be tied to building out GPU & CPU infrastructure, outfitting the large-scale data center investments over the last two years.

Overall, I’m long-term bullish on AMD stock. From a trading perspective, AMD has typically experienced volatility around earnings, presenting an opportunity to actively trade on the stock. For those seeking to amplify daily performance, AMDL can be utilized to enhance exposure with fewer dollars deployed.

The challenge afoot is AMD’s lagging position behind Nvidia. From a valuation perspective, AMD can be viewed as a substantially more expensive stock relative to NVDA, though it lags in some comparable multiples like price/sales. This may add certain risks with respect to the price level AMD shares trade at and, in turn, impact AMDL if investors see AMD as an expensive asset to hold.

Overall, trading AMDL will employ more of a momentum strategy with less emphasis on the fundamentals. I believe the TTM Squeeze can be utilized to determine buying & selling trends for AMDL where traders can buy into the upward momentum.

Risks Related to AMDL

AMDL is a leveraged strategy and may expose investors to certain risks that must be considered prior to making a final investment decision. AMDL provides 200% exposure to the daily performance of AMD shares by employing swap positions. Swaps are settled on a daily basis after market close, exchanging performance for a fixed rate. As a result of this, AMDL performance will experience a daily reset that can lead to a compounding effect on shares, amplifying upside or downside risk when held for longer than a single trading day.

Investing in leveraged strategies for long holding periods may result in value decay and unsalvageable losses. For this reason, I believe investors must exhibit strict risk management practices with set parameters for cutting losses.

Traders must also consider the total fees associated with trading AMDL. AMDL has a net expense ratio of 107 bps. In addition to this, traders will be exposed to short-term capital gains taxes that may impact total earnings on a trade. Though liquidity is presently ample for trading AMDL, traders must consider this feature when trading AMDL if liquidity were to tighten, potentially exposing them to spread risk.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

AMDL can be an appealing trading vehicle for those seeking to amplify exposure to AMD shares, providing 2x daily performance exposure. Traders should consider all risks associated with trading leveraged funds prior to entering into a position. Though I am long-term bullish on AMD shares, the market may be volatile in the near term, particularly as liquidity concerns arise. Given the risky nature of trading leveraged funds, I am recommending AMDL with a Hold rating.

data.title: Ross Stores: Q3 Strength Confirms This Retailer Still Knows How To Win
data.description:
When I went to the US earlier this year, I visited two Ross Stores (ROST). It's a business model that I find really interesting, and there's nothing like it here in Brazil. The appeal of it being practically a treasure hunt really works, creating that feeling that you'll eventually find a product you like at a really low price.

And what makes this possible are precisely the moats around the business model that the company has managed to build. It is a large network of merchants and stores, which is managed very efficiently with what the company calls “disciplined expense & inventory management.”

Q3 proved this, with the ROST stock rising 8.4%. But still, I prefer to be more conservative and buy when there is a better entry point, i.e., when the valuation is slightly better.

Q3 Earnings: Pretty Solid

Ross Stores' Q3 earnings were strong. The company surprised market expectations by $190 million in terms of revenue, and EPS was even more surprising, with Ross delivering $1.58, a beat of $0.16, or a surprise of more than 10%.

The guidance was also positive, with an outlook of $6.42 (midpoint) for 2025, while the market expected something closer to $6.25.

But in my view, the main thing was the execution in this quarter, which only proves that the business is strong and that management has vision (and in the long run, this matters much more to shareholder value than any beat or miss). In Q3, the company achieved comparable store sales of 7%. This is very strong; over the last 9 months, this indicator has been at 3%, and the guidance for Q4 is also close to this.

Having comparable store sales of 7% in a quarter that was not so easy for the economy, with many retail players struggling to maintain low single-digit growth, shows the strength of Ross Stores. In the earnings call, they attributed this strong performance to “compelling brand name assortments” and “new marketing campaigns.”

The operating margin for the quarter was 11.5%, with operating income of $648 million, impacted by tariffs and higher distribution costs, but offset by lower domestic freight and occupancy costs. Even with this very dynamic Q3, the operating margin improved, as it was just under ~10% in Q3 2024.

In short, I interpret ROST’s Q3 as a pretty solid quarter, both in terms of financials and outlook, which point to a solid business and gradual expansion.

ROST Valuation: Not Great

Although there are some risks, such as higher tariff costs that may occur in the coming quarters or poor execution in any quarter, like a worse inventory management (but management mentioned that it is healthy for the holiday season), the main risk to ROST stock, in my view, is its valuation.

It is not a bad valuation, but there is also no attractive margin of safety.

At first glance, ROST's valuation looks good. The company trades at a P/E of 25x, but as it maintains EPS growth and reliable prospects, it is not such a stretched multiple. Compared to its main peer, TJX Companies (TJX), these multiples also seem much more acceptable. Note how ROST is cheaper on virtually all metrics, even though it has similar growth.

But what bothered me the most was that in its presentation, the company shows a “potential” for 3,600 stores, versus the current ~2,200 (proxy), not that much if we run the numbers and think this may be a “full maturity.” But this also makes it easier to envision where Ross can be in the next 10 years or so.

If it continues at this pace, Ross might reach 3,600 stores by something near 2035. If revenue per store grows 3% per year, which is feasible considering inflation and some initiatives, we are talking about revenue of ~$50 billion by 2035. With a net margin of 10%, slightly higher than what it is delivering today, Ross would be trading at a P/E ratio of around 11x in 2035.

It really is a cheap multiple, as it would allow a dividend yield of 9% (on cost) if the company distributed all of this net income. Assuming that the exit multiple is 25x, we are talking about an upside of 123% in this period, but that in 10 years is about 8% per year. Another point is that I am not convinced that 25x earnings for Ross in 2035 is reasonable. On the one hand, it will be a more mature and established company, but at the same time, there will also be less room for expansion, and perhaps the market will want to pay less for the lower potential.

In other words, even if this scenario occurs, we are talking about only a reasonable return, around 8% per year or a little more depending on the premise.

The Bottom Line

Ross Stores is a strong company, and I like its business model and execution, which is why I believe it is a potential compounder if we are talking about the very long term. Still, it is not at a price that I consider attractive enough to initiate a position. Therefore, my initial coverage of ROST stock is a hold.

data.title: Eli Lilly: The Weight-Loss Leader With More Upside If Execution Holds
data.description:
Following my last article on Eli Lilly (LLY), the stock price surged by 47% and significantly outperformed the benchmark. I had a Strong Buy rating with a $800 price target, which was exceeded, and the company currently trades around its all-time high of roughly $1,030.

As I have anticipated, the obesity treatment market remained the key top and bottom-line driver. The company continues to be a pharma powerhouse in the weight-loss landscape, fueling a bullish narrative over the following years.

As volatility returned to the broader markets, Eli Lilly presented stability in business performance and a semi-defensive approach tied to the health landscape, which remains people's core priority. Therefore, LLY may appear to be a compelling diversification candidate, helping withstand market turbulence.

The healthcare companies of the S&P500 are still trading at a multi-year low valuation, highlighting an 18.40x earnings multiple, and I think a positive shift in sentiment may spark an end-of-year rally for the industry. If that happens, Eli Lilly appears to be well-positioned to benefit from improved sentiment. Therefore, my bullish thesis remains unchanged. I rate Eli Lilly a Strong Buy with a $1,165 price target over the next 12 months, highlighting a 14% upside possibility and broader market outperformance.

FQ3 2025: Phenomenal Quarter, Highlighting Eli Lilly's Dominance

On October 30, the healthcare powerhouse delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was an outstanding quarterly performance, highlighting accelerated top and bottom-line growth. The market reacted to the results with a 4% stock price appreciation.

Eli Lilly posted $17.6 billion in revenue, representing a 54% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $1.53 billion above analysts' estimates. The bottom line arrived at $7.02 in diluted EPS, reflecting a 5x surge over the past 12 months, and $1.13 above what the market had anticipated. If the company can continue delivering top and bottom-line growth at a high double-digit pace, I think the market may be willing to pay a premium for the company further.

In the earnings presentation, the healthcare powerhouse indicated that top-line growth was mainly driven by key products, representing $12 billion in revenue, highlighting a 104% surge. This portfolio consists of Mounjaro and Zepbound, weight-loss blockbusters, Kisunla, used for Alzheimer's, the cancer treatment drug Jaypirca, and others. This suggests that Eli Lilly remains a well-positioned healthcare powerhouse. A triple-digit revenue growth in the core portfolio is phenomenal and argues for the company's future success.

In addition to this, management emphasized its Kisunla drug has been approved in the EU, and Inluriyo, used for treating advanced or metastatic breast cancer, has been approved in the US, suggesting a positive tailwind and success in the anticipated launches. The company highlighted study results, indicating prolonged survival rates for their Vezenio, an early breast cancer drug, Jaypirca achieved a substantial improvement in progression-free survival in CLL/SLL treatment. The Orforglipron experienced successful results in the second obesity trial, and LLY has proceeded with a global regulatory submission in 2025.

This indicates that Eli Lilly remains a high-quality company, expanding its pipeline of innovations, which fuels a bullish narrative. The company posted $3.5 billion in research and development expenses, suggesting a 27% growth on a year-over-year basis, implying LLY positions itself to continue dominating the market over the following years.

In the press release, Eli Lilly management indicated that the US was the main region driving the top-line growth. The company posted $11.3 billion in revenue, highlighting a 45% increase over the past 12 months. This accounts for 64% of the company's total revenue. The revenue from international markets surged by 74% on a year-over-year basis, reaching $6.3 billion. Management indicated that Mounjaro was the main contributor outside the US.

Although it seems that Eli Lilly remains heavily dependent on the revenue derived from the US, international markets are gaining strong momentum. If the company can sustain such high double-digit international revenue growth further, it would become an even more diversified business, fueling a bullish narrative in the following quarters.

The company's diabetes and obesity treatment drugs continue delivering significant performance in the US. Mounjaro experienced a market share increase in both new-to-brand prescriptions and total prescriptions in the type 2 diabetes space, indicating a rise of 4 bps on a year-over-year basis. This highlights a 54% surge in NBRx and 45% growth in terms of Trx in the US, respectively. As I have indicated above, Mounjaro led top-line contribution internationally, signaling a 54% growth outside the US, which suggests that LLY positions itself to become a more globally diversified healthcare powerhouse.

The weight-loss blockbuster Zepbound continues its dominance in the US. The anti-obesity drug achieved a 63% market share in the US in FQ3 2025. Although this has represented a slight decrease by 2 bps, there are no structural issues, and LLY continues to dominate the weight-loss landscape. To support this, management revealed that Lilly's market share surged to 57.9% in the US. Furthermore, the global obesity treatment market was valued at roughly $16 billion in 2024 and is anticipated to quadruple by 2030, reaching $60 billion. This represents a 22.31% CAGR, and if LLY maintains a dominant stance, it will remain one of the key beneficiaries of the broader market growth.

The company continues a shareholder-friendly capital allocation approach through dividends and a share repurchase program. Eli Lilly returned $1.3 billion to its shareholders in dividends and $700 million in share buybacks. If the company continues such a shareholder-friendly capital strategy, the share repurchase program may support a significant double-digit bottom-line increase.

The healthace powerhouse achieved 82.9% gross profit margin in the quarter, which suggests significant fundamentals and outstanding business performance. The operating margin was 41.8% in FQ3 2025. I think $900 billion market capitalization company operating at such outstanding margins, and in parallel delivering a substantial top-line growth, deserves a premium valuation.

To conclude, this has been a phenomenal quarter for the pharma heavyweight. The company's key portfolio is driving both top and bottom growth, and dominates respective markets. Eli Lilly is growing revenue at a rapid pace in both the US and international markets, making it a compelling, well-diversified opportunity. In addition, LLY operates at premium margins, supporting a bullish narrative and positive outlook in the following years. I think it may seem to be an interesting opportunity for long-term investors seeking pharma exposure.

Although Eli Lilly Trades At A Premium, It May Be Cheap If Growth Delivers

I kindly invite you to read my previous article, highlighting a more in-depth valuation analysis of the company. Regardless, I would also like to note a few things.

Eli Lilly trades at a forward P/E of approximately 44, representing a 2.3x premium versus peers at 18.81x. The company is also trading at roughly an 80% premium versus the average company in the S&P 500 at a 23x earnings multiple. Although this may appear significant, the pharma powerhouse delivered a high double-digit revenue growth and triple-digit bottom-line growth in the last quarter; therefore, I argue the stock may be cheap if growth delivers.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 1.37, Eli Lilly is actually trading at a discount to peers at 1.77. This indicates a 23% undervaluation and supports my bullish thesis arguing for multiple expansion.

However, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 15.05 and 14.54, respectively, offset the bullish outlook and suggest a premium valuation. These valuation metrics reveal a 3.5x overvaluation at the midpoint versus the sector medians of 3.80 and 4.09. This signals that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth; therefore, even if LLY continues to deliver such phenomenal top-line growth, there is a chance it will not translate into meaningful stock price appreciation. On the other hand, it could also indicate that investors are willing to pay a substantial premium for the company's business and dominant market position.

Over the past 12 months, Eli Lilly posted 45% revenue growth. This implies a 7.5x outperformance versus peers at 6% and a 4.5x outperformance versus the benchmark at a historical 10% top-line expansion. LLY delivered 121% bottom-line growth, and that represents a massive outperformance versus the sector median of 17%, and 12x outperformance versus the average company in the S&P 500 at approximately 10% bottom-line growth.

Therefore, although the company delivers a significantly more rapid top and bottom line growth, it trades at roughly 2x premium versus both the industry and the benchmark. I think this supports my thesis that Eli Lilly may actually remain cheap if it continues to perform at such robust levels.

I kindly invite you to read my previous article on the LLY stock. Nevertheless, to conclude, Eli Lilly remains a phenomenal top- and bottom-line growth story, justified by massive demand and market dominance. Despite rapidly growing business fundamentals, Eli Lilly maintains elevated margins, validated by gross profit margin (TTM) of 83% and net income margin (TTM) of 31%. These metrics argue for a bullish narrative.

In addition, the pharma company is tied to the obesity treatment market tailwinds, and now, with the drug pricing agreement reached with the US, Eli Lilly has reduced uncertainty tied to tariffs and their potential impact. Although the deal may affect margins in the short term, LLY has pricing power; therefore, it should not cause structural risks. I believe Eli Lilly remains a high-quality, dominant player in the operating landscape, and it may appear to be a compelling diversification candidate.

Wall Street is estimating $23.68 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $32.11 in diluted EPS for FY2026. I agree with the market's expectations, as I believe Eli Lilly remains well-positioned to achieve these targets. I also believe the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line performance in the following quarters.

Although I believe Eli Lilly deserves a significantly higher earnings multiple, I favor a more conservative approach. Therefore, if we apply a forward P/E of about 30 to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $32.11, we arrive at my price target of $1,165. I think the strong momentum in demand may continue further, and that should fuel top- and bottom-line growth.

Eli Lilly Is A Healthcare Powerhouse, And My Bullish Thesis Remains Intact

I think Eli Lilly remains a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking diversification into the healthcare sector. The industry trades at multi-year lows; thus, once sentiment improves, it could act as a tailwind for LLY. Although the stock trades at a premium, based on my analysis, it could actually be cheap if growth delivers.

The risks and concerns remain as highlighted above, and in my previous article. Eli Lilly remains a moderately leveraged business with $42.58 billion in total obligations, and that could partially cloud the outlook. The company remains heavily dependent on GLP-1 blockbusters' performance, and sentiment may shift if demand weakens. Nevertheless, I argue that tailwinds outweigh the headwinds, and Eli Lilly remains well-positioned to dominate the pharma space and benefit from broader market growth.

Therefore, my bullish thesis remains intact. I rate Eli Lilly a Strong Buy with an increased price target of $1,165 over the next 12 months. This reflects a 14% upside possibility and broader market outperformance. 30 Wall Street analysts imply a $1,012 price target over the following year, indicating a nearly 4% downside potential. Although this is definitely a possibility, I think the stock is likely to mirror bottom-line performance, and if a rapid pace is sustained, it may appreciate. This is justified by management's excellent execution, strong market position, solid momentum in demand, and tailwinds tied to its future growth. Time will tell, but it will be interesting to monitor if projections play out.

data.title: Meta: Good Time To Buy The Social Media Winner
data.description:
Introduction

Meta Platforms (META) is one of the interesting cases that shows AI has not yet played a significant role in the secular growth of domain businesses. The company's stock price has been punished for what appears to be a likely perceived AI weakness, as well as the so-called AI big capex spending. The recent Meta quarterly report shows Meta has grown revenue by around 26% while keeping the high gross margin of over 83%. These numbers have been achieved with all the "AI misses" and are much better than those of AI leaders (achievers) like Google and Microsoft, etc., as we'll discuss in more detail later. The stock price has been down -21% since October 29, when the Q3 earnings were announced. I believe the stock is a good buy because Meta's still the social media giant in this secularly growing sector, evidenced by the competitors like YouTube and TikTok. AI or not, the organic growth prosperity still looks very promising and is proven once again by its Q3 report.

Meta Platforms Overview

Meta is a social media giant through its several major platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. Its large market share and ownership of those social platforms confirm its status as a dominant force in the industry. In fact, there is an antitrust monopoly lawsuit filed against Meta for the same reason. It is interesting to note that the lawsuit has been ruled in favor of Meta based on the company's claim of "declining in popularity" amid the formidable competition, such as TikTok and YouTube.

However, META's recent Q3 report does not show any signs of "declining". The organic growth of the social media relevant to Meta's core business has grown at a respectable rate, as summarized below:

Keep in mind that the revenue growth is at 26%, reaching $51 billion per quarter. The growth rate is much higher than the 5-year average (19%), as shown below.

On the other hand, the gross margin remains higher than 80%, which is a strong foundation to keep the company highly profitable. The operating margin was 40.1% in Q3 2025, a decrease from 42.7% one year ago, due to increased costs and expenses such as AI investments.

The company's stock price has suffered a big loss of over 21% post Q3 earnings, as shown below

One of the reasons to blame is that the reported GAAP net income ($2.71 billion) was an 83% year-over-year decrease, significantly impacted by a one-time, non-cash tax charge of $15.93 billion. Excluding the charge, non-GAAP net income would have been $18.64 billion, more in line with previous quarters. I believe this charge has no impact on the plans for the future, such as revenues and earnings. Any related effect on the stock price could be short-lived.

The bullish earnings cycle remains resilient.

META is considered one of the hyperscalers, which typically have billions of users. META's powerful vision targeting "human connection" is probably covering the biggest potential market amongst billions of consumers. I think the real moat for META resides in its applications, which are truly hyper-scaler, including the following data reported recently:

  • 3.5 billion people use at least one of the apps daily.
  • Instagram features 3 billion monthly active users.
  • Threads (a text-based social app) has over 150 million daily active users.
  • Video time on Instagram has grown more than 30% since last year.
  • Reels (a short-form video) sees an annual run rate of over $50 billion.

With one of the best product management teams and the founder-led execution environment, I don't really see a real chance of anyone taking over in the next decade or so in the natural course of the evolution for META's envisioned "people connection".

Notice that the impressive growth reported amid AI struggling (more to be discussed in the next section) has implied that the remarkable topline growth is really organic to its core businesses.

AI Capex Spending Myth - Where is the next innovation

Meta AI seems to face some product challenges, including a lack of catalyst for the competitive AI products, and ethical and safety concerns surrounding its chatbots. Difficulty in retaining top AI talent could also bring some uncertainties for its future AI. I have been looking forward to seeing a new and exciting AI roadmap from META for quite some time, but I will have to wait a little more time before it happens.

Despite such perceived AI weakness compared to the other hyperscalers, such as Google, etc., Meta Platforms is doubling down on AI investment in the compute infrastructure and next-generation engagement products. In fact, META has been raising its CapEx and expense outlook for both 2025 and 2026.

One of my favorite indicators for technology growth is the R&D (research and development) spending. A significant portion of AI spending is likely considered R&D for both accounting and tax purposes. R&D is critical for a technology company to keep its technology leadership, build/keep a moat, and facilitate the rapid cycle and not be disrupted.

I want to see META continue to raise AI spending, which could lead to a potential AI growth catalyst in the future. Just like what the CEO Zuckerberg stated in the Q3 earnings conference, that

"I am very focused on establishing Meta as the leading frontier AI lab. We're heads down developing our next generation of models and products, and I'm looking forward to sharing more on that front over the coming months."

I don't see much difference from the other hyperscalers in terms of AI visions, plans, and, more importantly, the spending. On the other hand, I could imagine the stock would sell off more if any of the hyperscalers were an outlier to announce a cut in the AI capex spending instead. AI can play the other way and crush the stock price like a bubble burst if AI gets "hyped" too much and does not see the expected outcome.

A safer Mag-7 stock to own

Meta's stock is selling off after its recent earnings report due to a combination of a large, one-time tax charge and concerns about the company's "aggressive" and significantly increased capital expenditure for artificial intelligence (AI) in 2026.

With all the negative sentiment built into META and the -21% price drop, the META stock may be less vulnerable to an AI bubble burst than the other current AI leaders, like Microsoft and Google. In other words, META would already be priced in a good "AI correction," and the AI fiction has already been largely removed. As discussed earlier, the AI could be becoming the tool for META to figure out the next-gen products, which will turn the AI spending into a tailwind instead of part of the walls of worry (e.g., alarm bells, etc.) about the AI capex spending for the hyperscalers.

On the other hand, META has now become a very attractive growth play amongst the mega-cap names. Below shows some of the metrics illustrating that META has the highest margin, highest revenue (market) growth, and lowest PEG ratio.

I don't see any reason META should be priced this cheaply based on the organic growth mentioned earlier. In fact, the higher margin with high growth is the right recipe for the multiple expansion and rewarding a much higher valuation. I see a potential price recovery of about 20% bringing META to the "fair valuation" of such a peer group.

The following is the earnings estimate for the next few years. Based on a similar growth trajectory, META would see PE in its teens in 2026.

Buffett has recently bought a chunk of Google shares, accounting for about 1.3% of Berkshire's portfolio. Now, based on the comparisons shown above, Google appears to be much more expensive than META. I think META is a better BUY at this level. I have opened a significant position recently and expect META may see a good rebound if we have a traditional year-end rally in the market.

Risk Analysis

The AI growth scare may continue to have an impact on the META stock price in the short term. There is also a risk that the multibillion dollar capex spending may not bring the AI or organic growth as discussed above to fruition. As a result, the growth projection would likely be cut and cause the stock to go even lower.

The regulation challenges have always remained a key issue in recent years. There is no guarantee the situation will be improved in the future. In other words, there could be a probability of hitting big negative news on that front, and making the stock price highly volatile.

Closing

The recent big drop in the META stock price does not seem to stem from worries about its future growth. The reported big write-off has no impact on the balance sheet, which remains strong. Meta's increased capex spending is a must-have R&D spending for future growth in the technology world, regardless of AI or whatever will come in the future. I see a big buying opportunity for META for the long-term investment, as the stock is in a deep oversold situation with RSI reaching well below 30 right now. We may see a possible price rebound should the bull market sentiment continue in the rest of 2025.

data.title: Celestica: The Overlooked Cisco Parallel
data.description:
The fact that Celestica's (CLS) share price has increased by 60% since my previous bearish call means the stock has become even more overvalued. Yes, the takeaways from my previous multiples analysis did not age well. Therefore, this time I invested my time into building a DCF model with two different scenarios to demonstrate how significantly CLS is overvalued. Only extremely aggressive growth assumptions like an 8% perpetual growth rate justify Celestica's current $50 billion valuation. While history suggests that such a perpetual growth rate is not impossible, as companies like Microsoft and Apple maintain double-digit revenue growth for decades, Celestica is not a company that writes a script and creates new "Blue Oceans". The company is extremely good at capitalizing on AI tailwinds, but I think that Celestica is more likely to repeat Cisco's growth trajectory rather than Microsoft's or Apple's. Of course, Celestica might soar further in 2026 if there will be new AI infrastructure budget boosts from the largest tech companies. However, betting on it when they have already increased their CAPEX budgets to around $100 billion each is pure gambling rather than value investing. All in all, I reiterate "Strong Sell" for CLS.

CLS' massive overvaluation

CLS is up by 239% YTD, bringing the company's market cap to almost $50 billion. If we look at the valuation ratios, they are extremely high compared to the company's historical averages. I said the same in my previous CLS article and even highlighted that Celestica's valuation ratios mirrored Nvidia's. However, since my previous bearish call did not age well, I think that I must change my valuation approach this time. Therefore, I am returning to the good old DCF model that did not let me down when I found out that CLS was undervalued several quarters ago. Since total debt constitutes only around 2% of Celestica's capital structure, I think that cost of equity is a suitable metric to be used as a discount rate.

According to my calculations using the CAPM formula, Celestica's cost of equity is 10.7%. This is quite high compared to the current risk-free rate, but I think it is fair due to the stock's extremely high volatility, with the last 52-week share price range spanning from $83 to $512.

After the discount rate is figured out, we can proceed with other DCF assumptions. Let's start with the one with the highest certainty - the free cash flow [FCF].
